scenario: Attorney asks: 'You're just lying because you hate my client, aren't you?'
feedback: Attorneys shall not ask argumentative questions that badger the witness.

scenario: Witness: 'My neighbor told me he saw the car speed away from the scene.'
feedback: This is an out-of-court statement offered for the truth of the matter.

scenario: Attorney: 'What was the driver thinking when they decided to hit the brakes?'
feedback: A witness may not testify to a matter unless they have personal knowledge of it.

scenario: Attorney: 'Isn't it true you were arrested for shoplifting ten years ago?'
feedback: Character evidence is generally not admissible to prove a person acted in accordance with that trait.

scenario: Witness: 'I really enjoy gardening on the weekends.' (In a trial regarding a bank robbery)
feedback: Evidence must have a tendency to make a fact of consequence more or less probable.

scenario: Attorney (on Direct Examination): 'You saw the defendant hold the gun, right?'
feedback: Leading questions are generally not allowed on direct examination.

scenario: Witness: 'The weather was beautiful.' (When asked if they saw the crash happen)
feedback: The witness's answer fails to address the specific question asked.

scenario: Witness: 'The victim screamed "He has a knife!" right as the door broke.'
feedback: This is an Excited Utterance, made while under the stress of a startling event.

scenario: Attorney: 'When did you stop stealing from your boss?' (No evidence of prior theft has been presented)
feedback: The question includes facts that have not yet been proven in the trial.

scenario: Attorney: 'Tell the jury your life story starting from the day you were born.'
feedback: Questions must be specific and not call for a broad, rambling narrative.
